title: The Ten Lepers
subtitle: Ten unclean men cry from afar, and one returns to give thanks

stillness: The Lord is near to all who call upon him in truth.
Stand here, far off, ashamed of your uncleanness — and yet draw near by praying.
gospel: And it came to pass, as he was going to Jerusalem,
he passed through the midst of Samaria and Galilee.
And as he entered into a certain town,
there met him ten men that were lepers,
And they lifted up their voice, saying:
Jesus, master, have mercy on us.
And when he saw them, he said:
Go, show yourselves to the priests.
And it came to pass, as they went, they were made clean.
And one of them, when he saw that he was made clean, went back,
with a loud voice glorifying God;
and he fell down on his face before his feet, giving thanks.
And this man was a Samaritan.
Were not ten made clean? And where are the nine?
behold: He had turned his back on Galilee and set his face toward Jerusalem.
He did not wander here and there, but held his road straight to the city where he must be slain.
He went through the midst of Samaria and Galilee — through the Gentiles and the Jews —
passing between them as a mediator, to make peace between those who were at discord.
And as he came near a certain town, ten leprous men met him, for they had heard the report of his wonders.
By the rite of the Law of Moses they were as unclean — shut out from the towns, unable to dwell with others.
The fellowship of their suffering had made them of one mind, and together they waited, anxious, for Jesus to pass.
They stood afar off, both out of reverence for Christ and lest they should infect the men who drew near.
And, as Theophilus says, they stood far off as if ashamed of their uncleanness, thinking he would loathe them as others did.
So they stood apart in place, but they were made near by their pleading.
And they lifted up their voice out of the greatness of their longing, and cried: Jesus, master, have mercy on us —
for by your word and command alone you are able to heal us.
If Jesus, then he wills; if master, then he is able; if they cry for mercy, then they show themselves wretched and in need.
And as they went to show themselves to the priests, they were made clean — by no power of the Law, but by the command of God.
consider: Only one returned, glorifying God with a loud voice.
He counted his healing to the power of Christ, not to himself.
Grateful, he fell on his face before the feet of Jesus, and adored him.
And he was a Samaritan — a Gentile, a stranger.
The cleansing already received gave him the boldness to draw near.
But the nine, who were Jews, remained ungrateful, and did not return to give thanks.
They credited their healing to the keeping of the Law, and not to the power of Christ —
though at the first they had begged him, faithfully and devoutly, to be healed.
The Lord does not ask as one who does not know;
he seeks the ungrateful as though ignorant of them: Where are the nine?
Consider, then, your own forgetfulness — how many gifts received, how few thanks returned.
prayer: Lord Jesus Christ, trusting in your tenderness,
I, like a leper, sprinkled over with the stains of many sins, flee to you, the merciful physician;
I, unclean, befouled with the spots of my vices, run to the fountain of mercies,
and as a suppliant I beg you: deign to heal my sickness, to wash away my foulness, and to guide me to salvation.
Grant that I may always hold your benefits in my heart, and give you thanks for all things;
but because I am a mortal man, ash and dust,
and cannot render even one thanks for a thousand,
let the Virgin Mary and all the citizens of heaven render you thanks for me,
and every creature that can be matter for the divine praise. Amen.

latinJewel.latin: Sic ergo abstiterunt loco, sed facti sunt proximi deprecando.
latinJewel.english: And so they stood apart in place, but they were made near by their pleading.
